front: War: a 19-year 12th-century English/Norman civil war fought between Stephen of Blois and Empress Matilda, also called the Nineteen-Year War or Nineteen-Year Winter
War: took its name from the lawless nature and lack of governmental presence that characterized the period
War: led to the formation of the House of Plantagenet through the eventual accession of Henry II
Event: the 1120 sinking of this vessel on a return voyage from Normandy drowned William Adelin, Henry I's only legitimate son, sparking The Anarchy
Battle: besieged here during a snowstorm, Empress Matilda escaped by dressing in white and crossing the frozen River Thames (the Isis) to camouflage herself against the snow
Figure: castle to which Empress Matilda fled after her escape from the Siege of Oxford Castle
Battle: during the Siege of Oxford Castle, one side placed siege engines atop this hill to bombard the castle
Battle: Empress Matilda's commander Robert of Gloucester was captured here, and later exchanged for the captive King Stephen
Battle: David I of Scotland invaded northern England in support of Empress Matilda, but the elderly Archbishop Thurstan led English forces to victory
Battle: took its name from a mobile, flagpole-bearing cart carrying a consecrated host and saints' banners (similar to the Italian carroccio) that marked the center of William of Aumale's line
Figure: baron whose pre-battle speech at the Battle of the Standard is recorded in an account by Aelred of Rievaulx
Battle: King Stephen was surrounded by Angevin knights and taken prisoner by William Kahmenes at this 1141 battle, briefly allowing Empress Matilda to rule
Battle: Stephen's capture and imprisonment here led to his later exchange for Robert of Gloucester, who was captured at the Rout of Winchester

Figure: switched sides after his property, including Walden Castle, was seized and he was arrested; his repeated side-switching and outlaw activity may be referenced in the Peterborough Chronicle's eyewitness account
Figure: supported one force during The Anarchy but changed his mind after his nephew Jordan de Bussey was abandoned at the fortress of Wark
Event: this baron's rebellion took place during The Anarchy, alongside the construction of many unauthorized "adulterine" castles chronicled by Robert of Torigni
Figure: began construction of Scarborough Castle during The Anarchy, which was forfeited once the war ended
Event: after The Anarchy had already concluded, a disgruntled commander plundered church lands near this town, where he choked to death on his meal
Document: this chronicle's final continuation laments that "men said openly that Christ and his saints slept: we suffered for nineteen winters for our sins"
Document: this historical work was the main text focused on The Anarchy, though it breaks off unfinished due to its author's death
Document: this work by William of Newburgh, covering English affairs from 1066 to 1198, is a prime source for The Anarchy
Treaty: ended The Anarchy in 1153 by letting Stephen remain king for life while recognizing Empress Matilda's son, Henry of Anjou, as his heir
Figure: King Stephen's son, whose attempted installation as king was rejected by the Catholic Church
Event: during The Anarchy, one side lost these two towns even after the peace agreement was signed at Wallingford
